intro.title: Brest to Vanikoro
intro.body: Jean-François de Galaup, comte de La Pérouse, sailed with the Boussole and Astrolabe on a French rival to Cook's voyages. He sent his journals home from Botany Bay in 1788; the ships were wrecked at Vanikoro that same year and the fate of the survivors remained unknown until Peter Dillon found wreckage in 1827.

stops.name: Brest
stops.note: Departure 1 August 1785. Two purpose-built storeships disguised as frigates carry savants, gardeners, and the king's instructions in sealed packets.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Breton roadstead at midsummer, two French frigates being warped out by ship's boats, dockyard cranes behind.

stops.name: Cape Horn
stops.note: Rounded January 1786 in unusually mild weather. La Pérouse notes the latitude in his journal with relief.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Towering black headland in heavy seas, two ships running before the wind with reefed topsails.

stops.name: Sakhalin
stops.note: Summer 1787. La Pérouse demonstrates Sakhalin is an island by sounding through the strait that bears his name to this day.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Fog-bound North Pacific island coast, ship's boat sounding a narrow strait with a lead-line.

stops.name: Botany Bay
stops.note: 26 January 1788. La Pérouse arrives as the British First Fleet is landing. He sends his last journals home by the British storeship Sirius.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Two French frigates passing British transports at anchor, a single ship-of-the-line raising the Union flag on shore.

stops.name: Vanikoro
stops.note: Both ships wrecked on the reef early 1788. Wreckage and survivors' tools were found by Peter Dillon in 1827 and Dumont d'Urville in 1828.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Reef-ringed tropical island under storm, broken spars and an anchor visible on the coral, no other ships in sight.
